title: Poem and Prayer for Invading Army
rights: public domain (presumed; pre-1963 broadcast, no rights statement on archive.org item)
transcript: We take you down to Hollywood, but we bring you Ronald Coleman with an exclusive feature in the NBC presentation of the events which are shaping history this day. Now, Mr. Coleman, especially for this broadcast over NBC, Miss Edna St. Vincent Millay has written a poem and prayer for an invading army. They must not go alone into that burning building which today is all of Europe. that you go with them, spirit and heart and mind. Although the body, grown too old to fight a young man's war, or wounded too deeply under the healed and frightened scars of earlier battles, must remain behind. You too may not be with them, save in spirit, you so greatly needed here, here in the very van in front of duty, to fashion tools and engines, and to engineer their transports, build the ships and mine the coal, without which all their efforts would be worse than vain. You, men and women, working in the workshops, working on the farms, makers of tools and of engines, fitters of wings to metal birds which have not left the nest, which yet must try their flight. Sores of seed and season, and planters of little plants, at intervals, on acres newly plowed and discred and harrowed to feed a starving world. You workers in the shipyards, building ships which crowd each other down the way, you miners of coal in dark and dangerous corridors, who see the sand total eclipse each morning, disappearing as you do under the earth's rim, not to emerge into the daylight until the days over and the light dim. All you, without whose constant effort, and whose skill, without whose loyal and unfailing aid, our men would stand stranded upon a foreign and hostile shore, without so much as a stout stick to beat away death or pain. Bullets like angry hornets, bising round the ears in the bewildered brain, and from the sky again and yet again the downpour of heavy evil, accurate murderous reign. You, who have stood behind them through desire, moves trung from behind them now. Let's till the weary bones encased the indefatigable will. But how can men draw near, so fierce a conflagration? And here, across a grey and cold and foggy sea, its heat is felt. Why touch your cheek? Is it not hot and tight and dry? And look what light climbs up the eastern sky and sinks and climbs again. Like to the brighter aurora of the north, it floods and flashes, pulses, pales. Then glows, lighting the entire east majestically, as if it were the sun that rose. I wish it were. Surely our fiber and sinew, the backbone and brain of us, are made of sunless, common stuff than clay. Surely the blood which warms the veins of heroes at the front, our brothers and our sons, runs also in our own. And are we not then capable, perhaps, of something more courageous than we yet have shown. Surely some talisman, some token of our love to pride in them, our heavy gratitude and so much so much love will find its way to them. Some messenger, the vicar and the angels of what we feel, will fly before them where they fly, before them and above. Like patron goddesses in wars of old, cleaving with level, lovely brows, the hard air before the eager prowls, lighting their way within contestant wings and winged heel. This is the hour. This is the appointed time. The sound of the clock falls awful on our ears. As the sound of the bells, their metal clang and chime, tolling, tolling for those about to die. But we know well they will not all come home to lie in summer on the beaches, and yet weep not you mothers of young men, their wives, their sweet hearts, or who love them well, fear not the tolling of the solemn bell. It does not prophesy, and it cannot foretell, it only can record. And it records today the passing of a most uncivil age, which had its elegance, but lived too well and far, oh far too long, and which on history's page will be found guilty of injustice and grave wrong. Oh thou, thou prince of peace, this is a prayer for war. not a war of man against his fellow-man, say, rather, Lord, we do beseech thy guidance and thy help. In exercising from the mind of man where she has made her nest a hideous and most fertile beast, and this to bring about with all his patch, for look, were even now she would lie down again to wealth. Lord God of hosts, With our Lord of Hosts not only, not alone of battling armies, Lord and King, but of the childlike heart as well, which lungs to put away, oh, not the childish, but the adult, secure to send a droid and deep and violent thing called war, and sing the beauties of this late to come but oh so loveless spring, for see where our young men go forth in mighty numbers to set free from torture and from every jeopardy things that are dear to thee. Keep in thy loving care we pray, those of our fighting men whose happy fortune it may be to come back home again after the war is over, and all those who must perforce remain the morn, the valiant slain. This we besiege the Lord. And now, before we rise from kneeling, one thing more, suffer now hard and angry hearts, make us ashamed of doing what we do. The Nephi very eyes, knowing it does this please thee. Make us more humble, Lord, for we are proud without sufficient reason. Let our necks be bowed more often to thy will, for well we know what deeds find favor in thy sight, and still we do not do them. O Lord, all through the night, all through the day, keep watch over our brave and dear, so far away. make us more worthy of their valor. And I love. Let them come home. Oh, let the battle, Lord, be brief, and let our boys come home. So cries the heart sick for relief from its anxiety and seeking to foster all a certain and a greater grief. So cries the heart aloud, but the thoughtful mind has something of its own to say. On that day, when they come home from very far away, and further than you think, for each of them has stood upon the very brink, or sat and waited in the anti-room of death, expecting every moment to be called by name. Now look you to this matter well. That they upon returning shall not find seated at their own tables at the head, perhaps, on festive board, trinked out in prodigal array, the very monster which they salad forced the conqueror into quail, and left behind for dead. Let us forget such words and all they mean as hatred, business, ranker, greed, intolerance, bigotry. Let us renew our faith and pledge to man his right to be himself and free. Say that the victory is ours, then say, and each must search his heart in true humility. Lord, Father, who are we that we should wield so greater weapon for the right and rehabilitation of our creature, man? Lo, for more corners of the earth, we ask or great and noble to come forth. Converge upon this errand in this task with generous and gigantic plans. Hold high this torch who will lift up this sword who can. You have heard Ronald Coleman in a poem and prayer for an invading army written especially for this broadcast by Miss Edna St. Vincent Malay. This first reading of the poem was an explosive feature in the NBC presentation of the World checking events of the day. This is the National Broadcasting Company. Having cancelled all regularly scheduled programs, NBC invite you to listen tonight at eight o'clock over most of these stations to Ginny Simms in a program especially arranged for this invasion day. tuned all night to NBC for the latest bulletins as they come from the fighting in front.
